First, I remember that prostaglandin eye drops, like latanoprost, work by increasing the outflow of fluid through the uveoscleral pathway. This increased outflow might lead to more movement of pigment from the iris to the conjunctiva, which could explain those dark deposits. 

Now, looking at the other options: carbonic anhydrase inhibitors (CAIs) usually reduce fluid production. They don't really involve moving pigment around, so maybe they aren't linked to these deposits. Beta blockers also decrease fluid production, similar to CAIs. My additional reasoning is that prostaglandins uniquely enhance uveoscleral outflow, promoting iris pigment migration to the conjunctiva—a mechanism absent in other glaucoma treatments like CAIs or beta-blockers, which do not induce pigment redistribution.
